methodologies.name: Feedback to Refinement Loop
methodologies.summary: Close the loop from production telemetry back to prompt and tool changes. Pipe traces, user signals, and outcome metrics into automated issue detection plus a human-in-the-loop review queue; route confirmed issues into prompt and tool refinement; re-validate the change through experimentation before it ships. The loop is the operating posture of a deployed LLM application — not a one-off improvement project — and it explicitly aggregates and prioritizes improvements so that engineering effort tracks user pain rather than developer intuition.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  prod[Production LLM app] --> tel[Telemetry: traces, user feedback, outcomes]
  tel --> s1[Build feedback pipeline]
  s1 --> store[(Queryable telemetry store)]
  store --> s2[Automated issue detection]
  s2 --> flagged[Flagged trace clusters with proposed root causes]
  flagged --> s3[Human-in-the-loop review]
  s3 --> decide{Confirm root cause?}
  decide -->|no| store
  decide -->|yes| backlog[Confirmed issue]
  backlog --> s5[Aggregate and prioritize]
  s5 --> ranked[Prioritized backlog by user impact]
  ranked --> s4[Refine prompts and tools]
  s4 --> change[Versioned candidate change]
  change --> s6[Re-validate via experimentation]
  s6 --> pass{Targeted metric up, no regressions?}
  pass -->|no| s4
  pass -->|yes| ship[Ship to production]
  ship --> prod

methodologies.example_scenario:
A legal-research SaaS has had its case-summarisation agent in production for six months. Telemetry flows into a Snowflake store: every trace, every thumbs-down with optional comment, every 'edited summary' that the lawyer-user kept after correcting. An automated detector runs nightly, clustering low-rated traces and proposing root-cause hypotheses against a fixed taxonomy (citation error, jurisdiction confusion, missing key holding, tone too informal).

Last week the detector flagged a cluster of 47 thumbs-downs with the proposed cause 'jurisdiction confusion — agent conflates federal and state authority on similar topics'. A domain reviewer (a junior associate the team contracts for triage two hours a day) confirmed 38 of the 47 as legitimate, ruled out 6 as user-side complaints unrelated to the agent, and marked 3 as uncertain pending more data. The 38 confirmed went into the backlog with a severity score derived from user-tier (paying enterprise vs free trial) and frequency.

This week the prompt team picked the jurisdiction issue as the highest-ranked item, drafted a prompt revision that explicitly asks the model to state jurisdiction before citing, and added a new tool that returns the canonical state-vs-federal classification for any case citation. They ran the candidate against a holdout of 200 historical traces and against a 5% live canary; the jurisdiction-error rate dropped from 8.2% to 1.7%, and no other metric regressed. Then it shipped. Crucially, the loop is the operating posture — nobody framed this as a 'project', it's just what the team does every week.
methodologies.intent: Convert production signals into prioritized prompt and tool changes that have been re-validated before they reach users.
methodologies.when_to_apply: An LLM application or agent is in production with real users, telemetry is being captured, and the team is responsible for sustained quality rather than initial launch. Don't apply pre-launch — there is no production signal yet, and the methodology degrades to a hypothetical pipeline. Exceptions: a closed beta with representative users and active telemetry can be treated as production for the purposes of this loop.

inputs.name: Production telemetry
inputs.description: Traces, latencies, tool-call records, completion outcomes, and structured user feedback from the live system.

inputs.name: Versioned prompts and tools
inputs.description: The current set of prompts, tool definitions, and configurations, each addressable by version for diffing and rollback.

inputs.name: Experimentation harness
inputs.description: Mechanism for running a candidate change against a holdout or a sample of live traffic before full rollout.

outputs.name: Prioritized issue backlog
outputs.description: Aggregated and ranked list of recurring failure modes drawn from telemetry and reviewer flags.

outputs.name: Refined prompts and tools
outputs.description: New versions of prompts, tool definitions, or guardrails motivated by specific issues in the backlog.

outputs.name: Validation report per change
outputs.description: Experiment result demonstrating the candidate change moves the relevant metric in the right direction without regressing others.

steps.name: Build the feedback pipeline
steps.description: Wire production telemetry — traces, user feedback, outcome signals — into a queryable store. Without a pipeline, the rest of the loop has no input.

steps.name: Automate issue detection and root-cause analysis
steps.description: Run heuristics or model-based detectors over telemetry to surface recurring failure modes, cluster similar traces, and propose root causes. Detection is automated; resolution is not.

steps.name: Human-in-the-loop review
steps.description: Domain reviewers triage flagged issues, confirm or reject root causes, and decide which warrant prompt or tool changes. Reviewer judgement is the gate between signal and action.

steps.name: Refine prompts and tools
steps.description: For confirmed issues, author prompt edits, new tool definitions, or guardrail changes. Each change references the issue and is versioned alongside the rest of the application.

steps.name: Aggregate and prioritize improvements
steps.description: Roll multiple related fixes into release candidates ordered by user impact, not by who proposed them. Engineering effort tracks the backlog ranking.

steps.name: Re-validate via experimentation
steps.description: Before full rollout, run the candidate change against a holdout or sample of live traffic. Confirm the targeted metric improves and no others regress. Then ship.

principles: Production telemetry is the only honest source of improvement priorities — developer intuition is downstream of it.
Detection automates; resolution does not — human judgement is the gate between signal and code change.
Every refinement is re-validated before users see it — shipping the fix is the last step, not the first.
Prioritization is global, not per-engineer — the backlog ranks user impact across the whole team's findings.

known_failure_modes.note: Telemetry is captured but never triaged — the pipeline exists, the loop does not close.
Every issue routes to human review with no decision criteria; refinements never ship because they're forever 'pending validation'.

sources.title: Building Applications with AI Agents
sources.quote: Feedback Pipelines, Automated Issue Detection and Root Cause Analysis, Human-in-the-Loop Review, Prompt and Tool Refinement, Aggregating and Prioritizing Improvements

methodologies.name: Automation Experience Uplift
methodologies.summary: Scale agentic AI in the enterprise by retrofitting agentic reasoning onto processes that are already automated rather than greenfielding new agent deployments. Mature 'Level 2' automation — RPA, BPM, deterministic pipelines — already carries the institutional knowledge, exception handling, and integration surface that agents need; layering agent reasoning on top converts them into 'Level 3' agentic systems faster and more safely than building agentic processes from scratch. The methodology treats the existing automation estate as the prior, not as legacy to be replaced.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  estate[Level-2 automation estate] --> s1[Inventory L2 automated processes]
  s1 --> inv[Inventory: owners, scale, exception rate, human-touch points]
  inv --> s2[Identify high-agentic-leverage candidates]
  s2 --> shortlist[Candidates: high exception or judgement load]
  governance[Transformation portfolio governance] --> s5
  shortlist --> broken{Underlying process sound?}
  broken -->|no| fix[Fix L2 first - do not uplift broken process]
  broken -->|yes| s3[Layer agentic reasoning on existing spine]
  s3 --> uplifted[L3 agentic process - deterministic core retained]
  uplifted --> s4[Validate against process-level metrics]
  s4 --> moved{Metrics moved vs pre-uplift baseline?}
  moved -->|no| rollback[Roll back agentic addition]
  moved -->|yes| s5[Scale across portfolio]
  s5 --> portfolio[Portfolio-level uplift roadmap]
  portfolio --> shortlist

methodologies.example_scenario:
A European bank already runs roughly 240 RPA bots across back-office operations — onboarding KYC checks, payment-exception handling, customer-correspondence routing, regulatory-report compilation. The Chief Automation Officer is now under pressure to 'do agentic AI' and the first instinct from the AI lab is to greenfield a new flagship 'agentic banking assistant'. The methodology pushes back: the bank's prior is 240 mature, integrated, governed automations, and that is where the agentic uplift will scale.

They inventory the estate. For each bot they note ownership, transaction volume, current exception rate, and where humans currently intervene. Payment-exception handling jumps out: the RPA bot processes 92% of incoming payments deterministically, but the remaining 8% — flagged for mismatched beneficiary names, unusual amounts, or routing-rule conflicts — go to a queue that takes humans an average of 14 minutes per case to resolve. That 14-minute judgement step is the agentic uplift opportunity.

They layer an agent on top: the deterministic RPA spine stays exactly as is, but exceptions are now routed first to an agent that has access to historical resolution logs and the bank's payment-rules knowledge base. The agent proposes a resolution with rationale; a human reviews. After three months, average exception-resolution time drops to 4 minutes, human-touch reduces 71%, and the deterministic 92% has not been touched. They roll the same pattern across KYC document-completeness (next highest leverage) and then correspondence routing. One candidate — regulatory-report compilation — was excluded after the inventory revealed the underlying process itself was broken (manual data hand-offs to the bot that the bot couldn't validate). The team explicitly chose to fix the L2 process first rather than ship an agentic broken process.
methodologies.intent: Scale agentic deployments across an enterprise by elevating existing automated processes to agentic operation, rather than building new agent systems from zero.
methodologies.when_to_apply: An organization already runs meaningful Level-2 automation (RPA bots, BPM workflows, scripted pipelines) and is now investing in agentic AI. The aim is breadth of agentic coverage across the enterprise, not the depth of a single flagship agent. Don't apply when the existing automation is poorly understood, undocumented, or itself a source of broken processes — uplifting a broken process produces an agentic broken process. Exceptions: a single high-value greenfield agent (e.g. a novel customer experience) can justify its own bottom-up build.

inputs.name: Inventory of Level-2 automated processes
inputs.description: Map of existing RPA, BPM, and scripted automation, with owners, integrations, and current performance.

inputs.name: Agentic capability targets
inputs.description: Statement of which kinds of reasoning, judgement, or exception handling agentic AI should add to each candidate process.

inputs.name: Transformation portfolio governance
inputs.description: Programme-level prioritization across the candidates, so uplifts compose into an enterprise transformation rather than a scatter of pilots.

outputs.name: Prioritized uplift roadmap
outputs.description: Ordered list of Level-2 processes targeted for Level-3 uplift, with expected impact and dependencies.

outputs.name: Level-3 agentic processes
outputs.description: Each completed uplift: the original automation retained as the deterministic core, agent reasoning layered on for judgement and exception handling.

outputs.name: Portfolio-level uplift metrics
outputs.description: Cross-process metrics — exception-resolution rate, human-touch reduction, cycle-time — used to manage the transformation portfolio.

steps.name: Inventory existing Level-2 automation
steps.description: Catalogue the automated processes already in production. Note ownership, scale, exception rate, and where humans currently intervene — these intervention points are the agentic uplift opportunity.

steps.name: Identify candidates with high agentic leverage
steps.description: Prioritize processes where deterministic automation handles the bulk volume but exceptions, judgement calls, or non-routine inputs still require humans. Those are the highest-leverage uplift candidates.

steps.name: Layer agentic reasoning onto the existing process
steps.description: Keep the existing deterministic automation as the spine. Add an agent that handles exceptions, makes judgement calls, or extends the automation to non-routine cases. Do not rewrite the spine.

steps.name: Validate uplift against process-level metrics
steps.description: Measure exception-resolution rate, cycle time, and human-touch reduction against the pre-uplift baseline. Roll back agentic additions that don't move these metrics.

steps.name: Scale across the portfolio
steps.description: Apply the same uplift pattern across the inventory. Treat the work as a holistic AI corporate transformation, not a sequence of disconnected agent pilots.

principles: Mature automation is a prior, not legacy — agentic AI adds reasoning to it, it does not replace it.
Uplift the highest-leverage processes first — exception-handling and judgement calls, not the deterministic core.
Don't uplift broken processes — fix the Level-2 automation first or you ship an agentic broken process.
Manage the work as a transformation portfolio — individual uplifts compose into enterprise impact only with cross-process governance.

known_failure_modes.note: Retrofitting agentic reasoning onto an automation that itself encodes a flawed process — the agent amplifies the dysfunction faster.
Replacing the deterministic Level-2 spine with the agent rather than layering on top — losing the predictable, auditable core.

sources.title: Agentic Artificial Intelligence
sources.chapter: Ch 12 'Scaling AI Agents: From Vision to Reality' (pp. 403–426), §'The Automation Experience Advantage: from Level 2 to Level 3 agents'
sources.quote: The Automation Experience Advantage: from Level 2 to Level 3 agents ... Leveraging Generative AI and AI Agents for a Holistic AI Corporate Transformation

methodologies.name: Production Failure-Mode Optimization
methodologies.summary: Optimize a deployed multi-agent system by walking a named checklist of recurring failure modes — vague instructions, wrong model size, prompt-model mismatch, weak tools, no stop criterion, wrong pattern, no memory, no metacognition, no evals, no human-delegation — and remediating each one with a targeted change. The methodology treats failure modes as optimization opportunities rather than incidents, and turns a fuzzy 'the system is underperforming' into a sequence of falsifiable interventions. It is checklist-driven and intentionally not exhaustive — covering the modes that empirically explain most production underperformance.

diagram.mermaid:
flowchart TD
  mas[Deployed multi-agent system] --> s1[Snapshot baseline performance]
  s1 --> base[Per-agent / per-task metric baseline]
  checklist[Failure-mode checklist] --> s2[Walk the checklist against traces]
  traces[Traces, tool calls, outputs] --> s2
  s2 --> labeled[Each mode: confirmed / ruled-out / uncertain]
  labeled --> uncertain{Uncertain modes?}
  uncertain -->|yes| more_tel[Collect more telemetry]
  more_tel --> s2
  uncertain -->|no| s3[Rank confirmed modes by leverage]
  s3 --> ranked[Ranked intervention list]
  ranked --> s4[Apply ONE intervention per cycle]
  s4 --> intervention[Single targeted change]
  intervention --> s5[Measure delta vs baseline]
  s5 --> accept{Targeted metric up, no regression?}
  accept -->|yes| s6[Re-baseline]
  accept -->|no| revert[Revert change]
  revert --> s4
  s6 --> done{More modes to address?}
  done -->|yes| s4
  done -->|no| stable[System optimized for this round]

methodologies.example_scenario:
A retail-ops team owns a four-agent product-content multi-agent system: a research agent gathers product specs from supplier feeds, a copy agent drafts marketing descriptions, a compliance agent checks claims against regulator-allowed language, and a coordinator agent assembles the final SKU page. Quality has been quietly drifting — internal reviewers report 'the descriptions feel off' and the compliance agent flags more than it should. Nobody can point to a specific bug. The team applies the checklist.

Baseline snapshot: per-agent success rates and per-SKU end-to-end pass rate over the last 30 days. Then the walk. Vague instructions — research agent prompt confirmed (it says 'gather useful spec info' with no schema); copy agent ruled out; coordinator uncertain. Wrong model size — copy agent confirmed (running on the smallest model; tone failures cluster there). Prompt-model mismatch — ruled out. Weak tools — research agent has only one supplier-feed tool; for products from EU suppliers a second tool is needed; confirmed. No stop criterion — coordinator confirmed (loops on compliance disagreements indefinitely). No metacognition — confirmed across the board. No evals — partially confirmed (only end-to-end pass rate; no per-agent evals).

The team ranks: vague-instructions and missing-stop-criterion typically explain the largest gaps and are cheap. Cycle 1: rewrite research-agent prompt with a strict schema; baseline-per-cycle metric (research-agent success rate) lifts from 71% to 89%; accept. Cycle 2: add a 5-turn stop criterion plus escalate-to-human on the coordinator; end-to-end pass rate lifts from 64% to 78%; accept. Cycle 3: upgrade copy agent to a stronger model; tone-failure rate drops; accept. Cycle 4: add the EU supplier tool; partial improvement but introduces a new latency regression on non-EU SKUs; revert and shelve for a follow-up cycle. Five cycles in, end-to-end pass rate is 87% with attribution per change. The team didn't add more tools to chase 'weak tools', conscious that tool-explosion is its own checklist failure mode.
methodologies.intent: Diagnose and remediate a deployed multi-agent system by walking a named failure-mode checklist and applying one targeted intervention per mode.
methodologies.when_to_apply: A multi-agent system has shipped, performance is below expectation, and the team needs a structured way to find what is broken rather than guessing. The system is observable enough that traces can be inspected per mode. Don't apply pre-deployment as a design checklist — the modes are diagnostic, not constructive; build with the relevant patterns first and use this methodology to triage problems in the running system. Exceptions: post-mortem of a closed beta with representative traffic.

inputs.name: Deployed multi-agent system with traces
inputs.description: Running system whose agent traces, tool calls, and outputs are inspectable per session.

inputs.name: Failure-mode checklist
inputs.description: The named list — vague instructions, wrong model size, prompt-model mismatch, weak tools, no stop criterion, wrong pattern, no memory, no metacognition, no evals, no human-delegation.

inputs.name: Performance baseline
inputs.description: Pre-intervention metric snapshot per agent and per system task, used to attribute remediations.

outputs.name: Mode-by-mode diagnosis
outputs.description: For each checklist mode, evidence from traces of whether it applies and how strongly.

outputs.name: Targeted interventions
outputs.description: One remediation per confirmed mode — clarified instructions, model swap, additional tool, added stop criterion, pattern change, memory addition, metacognitive loop, eval, human-delegation gate.

outputs.name: Post-intervention metric report
outputs.description: Performance delta per intervention, used to confirm or revert each change.

steps.name: Snapshot baseline performance
steps.description: Capture current metrics per agent and per system task. Without a baseline, no intervention can be attributed.

steps.name: Walk the checklist
steps.description: For each named mode, inspect traces and outputs to determine whether the mode applies. Mark each as confirmed, ruled-out, or uncertain — uncertain modes require more telemetry before action.

steps.name: Rank confirmed modes by expected leverage
steps.description: Some modes — vague instructions, missing stop criterion, wrong pattern — typically explain the largest performance gaps. Rank confirmed modes accordingly so the first intervention has the highest expected payoff.

steps.name: Apply one intervention per cycle
steps.description: Make a single targeted change per cycle — clarify instructions, swap model size, add a tool, install a stop criterion, change the pattern, add memory, add a metacognitive loop, add evals, add a human-delegation gate. One variable per cycle so the metric delta has a unique attribution.

steps.name: Measure, accept, or revert
steps.description: Re-run the eval or production sample. If the targeted metric improved without regressing others, accept the change. If not, revert and move to the next ranked mode.

steps.name: Re-baseline and continue
steps.description: After an accepted intervention, snapshot the new baseline before the next cycle. The checklist is not one-shot — modes can re-emerge as the system grows.

principles: Failure modes are optimization opportunities, not incidents — name them to act on them.
Diagnose from traces before changing code — assumptions about which mode applies are usually wrong.
One intervention per cycle — multi-variable changes produce uninterpretable metric deltas.
The checklist is intentionally finite — cover the modes that empirically explain most underperformance, accept that it isn't exhaustive.

known_failure_modes.note: Walking the checklist superficially — declaring modes ruled-out without trace evidence — produces no real diagnosis.
Responding to every 'weak tools' diagnosis by adding more tools, until the agent drowns in tool choice.

sources.title: Designing Multi-Agent Systems
sources.chapter: Ch 11 'Optimizing Multi-Agent Systems' §11.2–§11.3.12 'Failure Modes as Optimization Opportunities'
sources.quote: 11.3.1 Your Agents Lack Detailed Instructions ... 11.3.5 Your Agents Don't Know When to Stop ... 11.3.7 Your Agents Aren't Learning (Memory) ... 11.3.8 Your Agents Lack Metacognition ... 11.3.9 You Don't Have Evals for Your Tasks ... 11.3.10 Your Agents Don't Know When to Delegate to Humans
